Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty flexible and hands-on. I'd usually set aside chunks of time when I could actually do something practical with the material—like working through problems or creating my own summaries. With about 10 hours a week, I tried to spread it out, but I'm the type who learns better by doing rather than just reading. I'd often study in the library or common areas where I could move around and take breaks when I needed to.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed. With nine online courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. I tried to create some regularity, but life as an 18-year-old with extracurriculars doesn't always cooperate with perfect schedules.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—like an assignment deadline or if someone had replied to my discussion post. Sometimes my study group would mention something in the forums and I'd hop on to see what was happening. I also logged in when I got notifications on my phone, though I wasn't the type to just browse around aimlessly on the LMS.
Q4: It kind of became routine when I started connecting with peers through the discussion forums. Once I had people I was actually talking to regularly, logging in felt more natural—like checking social media almost. The peer support aspect really helped make it a habit because I genuinely wanted to see what others were saying and contribute to conversations. That social element made a big difference for me.
Q5: The video lectures were super helpful because I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. I watched over 150 minutes worth because visual demonstrations stick with me way better than just reading text. The discussion forums were also valuable because explaining things to others or reading their perspectives helped solidify my understanding. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything interactive felt more useful.
Q6: I'll be honest—I often skipped the longer reading materials or supplementary PDFs. They felt passive and I'd zone out trying to get through them. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it was easy to push it aside. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional quizzes because I was already stretched thin managing nine courses and my extracurriculars.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my grades or when it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. If I could apply it practically or discuss it with peers, that made it feel meaningful. I also valued activities where I could move at my own pace and engage actively rather than just passively consuming information.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which sometimes made them more enjoyable but also easier to skip. Required activities had this weight to them that pushed me to complete them even when I wasn't feeling motivated. With optional stuff, I'd only do it if I was genuinely curious or if my study buddies were doing it too. The peer aspect often determined whether I bothered with optional content.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work through them on my tablet during commutes or in places without great wifi. I also made physical flashcards and diagrams because writing things out by hand helps me remember better. My notebook was full of summaries and practice problems that never showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I was deep in study mode but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd meet up with classmates to work through problems together in person, or when I'd spend hours practicing concepts using materials I'd already downloaded. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay my downloaded version multiple times while taking notes. That repeated engagement wouldn't show up anywhere.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach for a lot of content. I'd access something once to download it, then study it extensively offline. Especially the video lectures—I'd download them and watch on my commute or before bed. It was more convenient and I could study without worrying about internet connectivity. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. Managing nine courses meant sometimes I had to make tough choices about where to focus. My attendance dropped to around 71% partly because of these overwhelming moments. But I've always been pretty resilient—I'd let myself feel stressed for a bit, then bounce back and figure out a plan to catch up.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or had multiple deadlines across different courses, my LMS usage would drop. I'd do the bare minimum—check what was absolutely necessary and log off. Fatigue especially affected me because I'm a hands-on learner and that requires energy. When I was running on empty, I couldn't engage the way I wanted to, so I'd conserve my effort for the most critical tasks.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd be logged in, maybe watching a video, but my mind would be elsewhere—thinking about other assignments or personal stuff. It's like my body was going through the motions but my brain wasn't actually engaged. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'active' on the platform.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on some assignments, which is probably why my completion rate wasn't as high as it could be. Sometimes I'd avoid tasks that felt overwhelming or unclear, even knowing they mattered. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I understood better or felt more motivated. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but not always before deadlines passed.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me—probably my biggest driver for LMS activity. I'd have these bursts of engagement right before things were due, cramming in submissions and checking requirements. Without deadlines, I might drift and focus on other courses or activities. They created structure that I needed, even if it meant my engagement looked inconsistent over time.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me. When I got positive feedback or saw good grades, it motivated me to stay engaged and put in more effort. Constructive criticism made me want to improve and I'd often revisit materials after receiving it. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and move on to other things. Timely, specific feedback kept me connected to the course.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation quite a bit. When modules were clearly organized with obvious connections between content and assessments, I engaged more consistently. The discussion forums being integrated helped because I could combine social interaction with learning. But with nine courses, I gravitated toward whichever ones had the clearest structure and most engaging peer communities.
Q19: Having video content really helped because it suited my learning style better than walls of text. The peer interaction features made engagement easier too—I genuinely enjoyed the social aspect. What made it harder was when there was too much content without clear prioritization, or when assignments felt disconnected from what we'd learned. Also, some interfaces were clunky which made quick check-ins frustrating.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce quality work. They might be watching videos, reading discussions, trying to learn—but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. It could also be someone who's anxious about submitting imperfect work, so they keep researching but never feel ready to turn anything in.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was pretty good even though my LMS engagement looked low and my assignment completion wasn't great. I studied a lot offline and with peers in person. Sometimes the students who seem inactive online are actually deeply engaged with the material through other means. The LMS just captures one slice of how learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when you accessed things and basic patterns of activity—like login frequency and time spent on videos. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture peer discussions that happen in person, the notes you take by hand, or how deeply you're actually thinking about the material. It's quantity over quality.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting genuine effort into learning it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks mechanically—it's being curious, asking questions, discussing with peers, and applying what you learn. Engagement is when the course matters to you beyond just getting a grade. It's an active mental and sometimes emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support and study groups that happened outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my resilience—how I bounced back after tough weeks or how I maintained a positive attitude despite stress. It also wouldn't show the hands-on practice I did offline, the connections I made between courses, or the genuine happiness I felt when concepts finally clicked. Those human elements matter most to me.
